Title: Using synthetic biology to overcome barriers to stable expression of nitrogenase in eukaryotic organelles
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Mitochondrial processing peptidase beta subunit; metallopeptidase component
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Coexpression causes undesirable cleavage of heterologous NifD, potentially abolishing nitrogenase function.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Reconstituted yeast MAS1/MAS2 MPP in E. coli efficiently cleaved the Su9 leader peptide reporter. 2. The same MPP cleaved wild-type NifD but not the NifD-R98P variant in the intact nif system. 3. Coexpression of yeast MPP abolished diazotrophic growth with wild-type nifD, implicating MPP as the causative barrier to stable NifD accumulation in mitochondria.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous operon reconstitution in E. coli; Su9-GFP processing assay; coexpression with nif cluster; Western blot detection of NifD cleavage; diazotrophic growth test
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts as the protease complex responsible for aberrant cleavage of mitochondrial-targeted NifD, thereby reducing nitrogen fixation capability.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Reduced nitrogen source for growth and nutrition
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: IPTG-induced MPP expression; mitochondrial targeting context via Su9 leader
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: MAS1 is essential in yeast, so direct yeast knockout validation was not feasible; function was demonstrated through reconstitution in E. coli.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Mitochondrial matrix in yeast; recombinant cytosolic expression in E. coli

Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Coexpression with MAS1 promotes cleavage of heterologous NifD, compromising nitrogenase assembly.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Together with MAS1, forms the yeast MPP complex reconstituted in E. coli. 2. The reconstituted complex processes Su9-GFP and cleaves wild-type NifD, but not NifD-R98P. 3. This establishes yeast MPP as the enzyme complex underlying the mitochondrial instability of NifD.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Reconstitution with MAS1 in E. coli; Su9-GFP cleavage assay; coexpression with nif cluster; Western blot; diazotrophic growth assay
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to MPP-dependent cleavage of wild-type NifD and loss of nitrogenase-supported growth.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Reduced nitrogen source for growth and nutrition
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: IPTG-induced expression; mitochondrial targeting context via Su9 leader
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Essential gene in yeast; tested through reconstitution rather than knockout.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Mitochondrial matrix in yeast; recombinant cytosolic expression in E. coli

Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Plant MPPs also cleave heterologous wild-type NifD, creating a major barrier to engineering nitrogenase in plant mitochondria.
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Nicotiana tabacum; Arabidopsis thaliana
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Reconstituted plant MPPs from Oryza sativa, Nicotiana tabacum, and Arabidopsis thaliana processed the Su9-GFP reporter in E. coli, confirming activity. 2. All three plant MPPs cleaved wild-type NifD from the intact nif system but failed to cleave the NifD-R98P variant. 3. This indicates that MPP-mediated NifD instability is likely pervasive in higher plants and supports use of R98P-like variants for plant organelle engineering.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Reconstitution of plant MPP subunits in E. coli; Su9-GFP processing assay; coexpression with nif cluster; Western blot detection of NifD cleavage
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Plant MPPs cause cleavage of wild-type NifD, whereas engineered R98P variants evade cleavage and are compatible with nitrogenase engineering.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Reduced nitrogen source for growth and nutrition
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: IPTG induction in E. coli; organelle-targeting peptide context
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In plants, MPP subunits are integrated into the cytochrome bc1 complex, but active processing was still reconstituted in E. coli.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Mitochondria in plants; recombinant expression in E. coli

Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Wild-type NifD is unstable in mitochondria due to MPP-mediated internal cleavage; some substitutions at R98 severely reduce nitrogenase activity, indicating stability-function tradeoff.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Internal deletion mapping; site-directed mutagenesis and saturation mutagenesis at R98; mitochondrial targeting in yeast with Su9 leader; Western blot stability assays; acetylene reduction assay; diazotrophic growth assay; reconstituted MPP cleavage assay in E. coli
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: As part of the MoFe protein catalytic component of nitrogenase, participates in ATP-dependent reduction of dinitrogen to ammonia.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GAL1 (yeast, inducible); native nifH promoter in operon-based nif cluster; Ptac used for MPP modules, not nifD
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Wild-type NifD from K. oxytoca is proteolytically cleaved after mitochondrial targeting, preventing stable accumulation in yeast mitochondria. 2. Residue R98 is the key determinant for cleavage susceptibility; deleting residues 91-100 or substituting R98 blocks degradation. 3. Saturation mutagenesis identified R98P as the best compromise, conferring protease resistance while retaining ~70% nitrogenase activity, with R98H and R98N retaining ~55%. 4. R98P-equivalent substitutions in NifD from multiple diazotrophs also prevent degradation in yeast mitochondria. 5. In reconstituted yeast and plant MPP systems in E. coli, wild-type NifD is cleaved while R98P variants are resistant, establishing MPP as the causative protease.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: R98 lies near FeMo-co and contributes to intra- and intersubunit interactions, including with NifK D516; thus engineering this site requires balancing stability in eukaryotic organelles against catalytic competence.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key catalytic structural subunit for MoFe protein assembly and activity; not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: R98P-like engineered nifD variants enable stable mitochondrial-targeted NifD accumulation while preserving substantial nitrogenase activity, supporting nitrogen-fixation function.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mitochondrial targeting with Su9 leader peptide; anaerobic conditions for nitrogenase assays; IPTG-induced MPP expression in E. coli
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Mitochondrial matrix when targeted in yeast; cytosol in reconstituted E. coli assays
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly required as catalytic structural component for formation and function of the nitrogenase MoFe protein that produces ammonia
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Mo nitrogenase catalytic core / MoFe protein module
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High value for engineering nitrogen fixation in crop organelles; editing or designing R98P-like variants can prevent organellar proteolysis and should be combined with other nif components for stable nitrogenase assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides biologically usable reduced nitrogen for growth and nutrition
